summary: In the 1930s, Elko County was really hard hit by the Great Depression. Many sheep men and cattle men struggled, and unemployment was very high. The Western Pacific Railroad, which had its terminal in Elko, cut back operations severely, leaving many railroad workers out of jobs. I was just a young man there, trying to find work during those tough times.

John E. Robbins was my neighbor and a longtime state senator from Elko County. He was a close friend who understood my struggles to find work during the Depression. It was through his help that I eventually got a job with the Nevada State Park Service in Reno. I approached him several times before he helped me land that position.

Reno was where I landed a government job during the Depression, working for the State Park Service. I was hired as a procurement clerk for the office there, handling supplies for a CCC camp at Fort Churchill. It was my first real introduction to government work, and I worked out of a spare office in the district highway offices in Reno.

The Nevada State Park Service was responsible for managing Fort Churchill, which had been declared a state park during the Depression. They petitioned for federal funds to restore the old fort ruins, and I worked as a procurement clerk supporting that effort. This job was part of the emergency conservation work funded by Roosevelt’s programs.

The Civilian Conservation Corps, or CCC, was a New Deal program designed to help young men during the Depression. The camps were modeled after army camps but with more lenient rules, housing 200 to 300 men who wore khaki uniforms. I was connected to the CCC camp at Fort Churchill, where they worked on restoring the old fort walls as part of the state park project.

Fort Churchill was declared a state park during the Depression, and there was a CCC camp there working on restoring the old fort walls. I was involved as a procurement clerk for the Nevada State Park Service, buying supplies for that camp. The restoration was part of a larger effort funded by federal emergency programs under Roosevelt.

The Great Depression hit Nevada, especially Elko County, very hard during the 1930s. Unemployment was widespread, and many industries like cattle, sheep, and railroads suffered greatly. It was during this time that I struggled to find work until I got a job through government programs connected to Roosevelt’s New Deal.

President Franklin D. Roosevelt launched programs to combat the Depression, including the Civilian Conservation Corps and emergency conservation work. His government spending initiatives made it possible for me to get a job with the State Park Service in Reno. The restoration work at Fort Churchill was funded through his administration’s efforts to provide employment.

The Western Pacific Railroad had its terminal in Elko and was hit hard by the Depression. Unlike the Southern Pacific, it wasn't a transcontinental line, so it suffered more and had to curtail operations significantly. Many Western Pacific railroad workers in Elko lost their jobs, which made competition for work very tough for young men like me.

The Southern Pacific was a transcontinental railroad that, unlike the Western Pacific, was less severely impacted by the Depression. While the Western Pacific curtailed operations drastically, Southern Pacific managed to fare somewhat better. This contrast affected employment in Elko, where Western Pacific workers were hit hardest.

During the 1930s, there were CCC camps operating out of Las Vegas as well as Reno and other parts of Nevada. Although I was not directly involved there, I knew that the CCC was active in Vegas, helping young men find work and gain new experiences during the Depression.

Ely was another Nevada location where CCC camps operated during the Depression. While my work was centered around Fort Churchill and Reno, I was aware that Ely and Elko also had camps helping young men through Roosevelt’s programs. These camps were part of a statewide effort to provide employment and conservation work.
